data.title: Perimeter Solutions wins $500M fixed-price US defense logistics contract
data.description:
• Perimeter Solutions (PRM) has secured a fixed-price IDIQ contract worth up to ~$500M to supply aqueous film-forming foam, used in firefighting operations.
  • It is a 5-year contract with no extension options.
  • The order period is expected to end on April 29, 2031.
  • The foam will be supplied to key U.S. defense users, including the Army, Navy, and Coast Guard.
  • Funding will come from FY2026–FY2031 defense working capital funds, while the contract is being managed by the Defense Logistics Agency.

data.title: TriNet reiterates 2026 revenue $4.75B-$4.9B while tracking to top half of EPS guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TriNet Group, Inc. (TNET) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "I'm pleased with our start to 2026." (President, CEO & Director Michael Simonds) "Our strong first quarter adjusted earnings per share up 25% over prior year reflect our disciplined approach to both repricing health fees and managing our expenses."
  • "Health fee repricing over the last year created a headwind for new sales and retention, including our January 2026 renewal, where attrition was about 2 points worse than prior year." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "Despite the impact of our January repricing, we expect overall 2026 retention to be better than full year 2025."
  • "New sales grew modestly year-over-year in the first quarter." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "For sales opportunities in the post proposal stage, we saw the time to close extend by about 15%."
  • "During the quarter, we completed the acquisition of Cocoon, an industry-leading employee leave management application aligned with our compliance-first approach." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "Cocoon should integrate seamlessly into our platform and address a significant customer pain point."
  • "Next, we announced partnerships powering TriNet Global and TriNet IT." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "TriNet Global powered through our partnership with Multiplier" and "TriNet IT powered through our partnership with Electric AI" were positioned as expansions to the offering.
  • "This March, we launched TriNet Assistant, an AI tool giving our customers and colleagues access to our HR expertise whenever and wherever needed." (President, CEO & Director Simonds) "Between March 31 and April 16, inbound volumes typically increase on average by 12%."
  • "Total revenues were $1.2 billion, declining 5% year-over-year in the first quarter as expected." (Executive VP & CFO Mala Murthy) "We finished the quarter with approximately 299,000 total WSEs, down 12% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • "We are reiterating our full year guidance." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "Revenue is performing in line with our forecast and our stronger than forecast Q1 insurance performance has had the effect of shifting our full year earnings expectations to the top half of our guidance range, assuming no significant uncontrollable event."
  • "For 2026, we continue to expect total revenues to be in the range of $4.75 billion to $4.9 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "Our professional services revenue guidance remains in the range of approximately $625 million to $645 million, and our ICR remains in the range of 90.75% to 89.25%."
  • "Our adjusted EBITDA margin stays in the range of 7.5% to 8.7% and GAAP earnings per diluted share are in the range of $2.15 to $3.05 and adjusted earnings per diluted share in the range of $3.70 to $4.70." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing of repricing and retention, management now said, "With our January renewals complete, all cohorts within our customer base are now priced in line with more historical practices." (President, CEO & Director Simonds)

Financial Results

  • "For the first quarter, GAAP earnings per diluted share were $1.90, and adjusted net income per diluted share was $2.48." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "During the first quarter, we generated $186 million in adjusted EBITDA, representing an adjusted EBITDA margin of 15.2%."
  • "We generated $149 million in net cash provided by operating activities and $123 million in free cash flow." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "Free cash flow benefited from the 2025 tax law changes and timing of cash tax payments."
  • "Insurance costs in the first quarter declined by 9% year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "As a result, our first quarter insurance cost ratio came in at 84% and over 4 points year-over-year improvement."
  • "Operating expenses were impacted by a $14 million restructuring charge as we right-sized the business for its current size and executed our ongoing talent optimization and automation strategies, including AI implementation." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy)
  • "We returned $71 million to shareholders across share repurchases and dividends." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "We also leveraged our cash generation to acquire Cocoon."
  • "Cocoon as a stand-alone product is expected to be modestly dilutive to 2026 adjusted earnings per diluted share and neutral to 2027 adjusted earnings per share." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy)

Q&A

  • Jared Levine, TD Cowen: asked whether March sales-cycle impacts were concentrated by vertical or client type; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "Yes, fairly broad-based" and added, "We saw a little bit more as you moved upmarket."
  • Jared Levine, TD Cowen: asked about Cocoon revenue opportunity and FY 2026 contribution; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "The primary benefit...is really about delivering better outcomes on these to our PEO clients first"; Executive VP & CFO Murthy: "the revenue contribution to this year is very, very modest."
  • Tyler Barishaw, Truist: asked if Cocoon was opportunistic and whether more M&A should be expected; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "we feel very fortunate to have such a strong franchise and such a cash-generative business" and said "inorganic is a lever that we can pull as well."
  • Daniel (for Andrew Nicholas), William Blair: pressed on ICR outperformance and guide conservatism; Executive VP & CFO Murthy: "The other half of the improvement was attributable to favorable development from 2025" and "that is a onetime benefit."
  • Daniel (for Andrew Nicholas), William Blair: asked when WSE declines trough; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "we can stabilize WSE count here for the balance of the year."
  • Brendan Biles, JPMorgan: asked what TriNet learned from the insurance repricing cycle and about AI-driven entrepreneurship; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "it's a risk-taking business" and said, "we've actually gone through and redone our rating system."
  • David Grossman, Stifel: asked where repriced clients go and what resonates in the broker channel; President, CEO & Director Simonds: "there hasn't been a big change in the distribution of where clients are going" and said brokers care "first and foremost about the experience and the value their client is going to get."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with probing on sales-cycle lengthening, ICR sustainability, WSE stabilization timing, and the practical implications of repricing; questions included "how we should think about the conservatism of the guide maintenance" (Daniel, for Andrew Nicholas, William Blair) and "where do these people go" (David Grossman, Stifel).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing discipline and one-time items; Executive VP & CFO Murthy cautioned, "one quarter does not make a trend" and reiterated, "we believe it is prudent to maintain our full year range."
  • Versus last quarter, management’s confidence language shifted from previewing launches ("we will launch TriNet Assistant" (President, CEO & Director Simonds, Q4 2025)) to reporting operating impact ("we launched TriNet Assistant" (President, CEO & Director Simonds, Q1 2026)), while analysts continued to focus on repricing aftereffects and durability of insurance performance.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance posture stayed consistent, with Executive VP & CFO Murthy reiterating the same 2026 ranges in Q1 2026 that were introduced in Q4 2025, while adding that Q1 insurance performance "has had the effect of shifting our full year earnings expectations to the top half of our guidance range."
  • Strategic actions advanced from planned integrations in Q4 2025 ("We plan to announce new capabilities...and leave of absence" (President, CEO & Director Simonds)) to executed moves in Q1 2026 ("we completed the acquisition of Cocoon" and "we announced partnerships powering TriNet Global and TriNet IT" (President, CEO & Director Simonds)).
  • Analyst focus remained centered on repricing, retention, and WSE trajectories, but Q1 2026 included more scrutiny on ICR outperformance drivers and guide conservatism following the 84% quarterly ICR disclosure.

Risks and Concerns

  • "Health fee repricing...created a headwind for new sales and retention" (President, CEO & Director Simonds), with management also citing "a volatile business and geopolitical environment" and "the macroeconomic environment does remain volatile" (Executive VP & CFO Murthy).
  • "We do not expect to receive more benefit from the prior year." (Executive VP & CFO Murthy) "It's still early in the year" and "things happen" in claims costs, which management tied to the decision to maintain the full-year ICR range.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a stronger-than-expected start, tying profitability to completed health-fee repricing, favorable insurance development that was characterized as one-time, and continued expense discipline including a $14 million restructuring charge. Leadership reiterated full-year guidance ranges, while stating that Q1 insurance performance shifted full-year earnings expectations toward the top half of the guidance range, and highlighted near-term execution catalysts including the Cocoon acquisition, TriNet Global and TriNet IT partnerships, and early operational impacts from TriNet Assistant.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: General Electric wins $107.8M order under a multi-year defense framework
data.description:
• General Electric (GE) secured a ~$107.8M delivery order to supply J85 aircraft engines to the U.S. defense sector.
  • This order is part of a long-term defense contract (5 years 6 months base, extendable by 4 years 6 months), specifically covering Performance Period 5 with milestone-based lot payments tied to engine deliveries.
  • The current order is a one-year contract that does not include any option periods.
  • The deal is funded through the FY2025 defense budget and working capital funds, with the Defense Logistics Agency being the contracting activity.

data.title: Stryker in rare double earnings miss; maintains 2026 guidance
data.description:
• For the first time since at least Q1 2024 results, Stryker reported misses on both the top and bottom lines in its Q1 financial results.
  • However, the medtech maintained its 2026 guidance: Diluted EPS of $14.90 to $15.10 (consensus $14.96). Organic sales growth is projected at 8%-9.5%.
  • Stryker was hurt in the quarter by just a modest increase in net sales in Q1: the $6B figure was only a 2.6% increase.
  • Adjusted EPS also fell year over year to $2.60 from $2.84.
  • Stryker ended the quarter (March 31) with cash and cash equivalents of ~$2.9B compared to ~$4B on Dec. 31, 2025.

data.title: YD Bio Limited GAAP EPS of -$0.12, revenue of $0.6M
data.description:
• YD Bio Limited press release (YDES): FY GAAP EPS of -$0.12.
  • Revenue of $0.6M (+17.6% Y/Y).

data.title: U.S. wheat reverses lower after surging to near two-year high
data.description:
U.S. grain futures reversed course to finish lower Thursday following a retreat in crude oil prices and forecasts of improving weather across the central U.S.

On the Chicago Board of Trade, wheat (W_1:COM) for July delivery fell 2.3% to $6.36 3/4 per bushel after hitting its highest price since June 2024 in the previous session, and corn (C_1:COM) for July delivery added 0.6% to $4.74 3/4 per bushel after rising to a near one-year high, but July soybeans (S_1:COM) slipped 0.2% to $11.95 1/4 per bushel after reaching its best level ​in more than six weeks.

In April, CBOT wheat gained nearly 7%, corn climbed nearly 4%, and soybeans rose 2%.

Brent crude surged Thursday to a four-year high exceeding $126/bbl on fears that the Middle East war could see renewed military conflict and lead to a protracted supply disruption that could hurt global economic growth, but prices later ​turned around and finished lower.

The strength of oil prices, which have been rising due to supply disruptions linked to the war, has lifted the soy complex and corn futures, as soybeans and corn are feedstocks for biofuel.

Early planting of U.S. soybeans and corn ‌has progressed ⁠well, and forecasts for drier weather for the U.S. Corn Belt through this weekend eased some market concerns that storms could delay seeding in some areas, Reuters reported.

ETFs: (WEAT), (CORN), (SOYB), (DBA), (MOO)

data.title: RTX lands $335M contract modification for SM-6 missile production boost
data.description:
• RTX (RTX) received a $335.11M contract modification to continue manufacturing, assembling, testing, and delivering Standard Missile-6 tactical missiles (all-up rounds) for the U.S. Navy.
  • The work is expected to be completed by May 30, 2030.
  • Funding comes from Navy weapons procurement budgets: $295.98M (FY2025) and $39.13M (FY2026), already locked in at the time of award, and will not expire this year.
  • The contract is handled by the Naval Sea Systems Command.
  • The stock traded 2% higher at $176.10.

data.title: TETRA outlines first bromine production in early 2028 with up to 75M pounds of annual capacity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: TETRA Technologies (TTI) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Despite the backdrop of one of the most tumultuous periods in the history of the oil and gas industry, we started 2026 with one of the strongest first quarter performances in the company's past 10 years," said (President, CEO & Director Brady Murphy), highlighting $156 million in revenue and $26 million of adjusted EBITDA.
  • Murphy said the company is maintaining its 2026 framework and geopolitical exposure assumptions: "given that this region has historically accounted for about 5% of the company's revenue, we do not expect an overall negative impact on our financial results," adding that U.S., Europe, and Latin America activity could offset Middle East reductions.
  • Murphy said the company’s bromine supply strategy and vertical integration are central to its 2030 plan, stating, "Our bromine plant project in Southwest Arkansas continues to proceed on time and on budget," with "first production at the start of 2028" and "an annual capacity of up to 75 million pounds."
  • "Completion Fluids & Products revenue of $92 million and adjusted EBITDA of $26 million increased 10% and 12%, respectively, relative to Q4 2025," said (Executive VP, CFO & Chief Commercial Officer Matthew Sanderson), while noting year-over-year declines tied to prior-year Neptune project timing.

Outlook

  • Murphy said, "we are maintaining our prior '26 guidance of single-digit revenue growth over 2025 with Completion Fluid margins between 25% and 30% and Water Flowback in the mid-teens."
  • On the Middle East, Sanderson said, "some of our Q2 2026 completion fluid sales in the Middle East could be delayed," while also pointing to offsetting demand: "we are seeing an increased number of spot sales inquiries from regions and customers we have not historically supported, which could more than offset any delays."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, management repeated the same 2026 margin bands and continued to exclude Neptune contributions from baseline expectations, while adding more explicit commentary on shipping delays and potential Q2 timing impacts in the Middle East.

Financial results

  • Murphy reported $156 million in revenue and $26 million of adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026, characterizing the period as one of the strongest first quarters in a decade when excluding the prior-year Gulf of America Neptune benefit.
  • Sanderson reported segment performance with "Completion Fluids & Products revenue of $92 million" and "Water & Flowback Services... revenue of $65 million," alongside "Adjusted EBITDA of $9 million" for Water & Flowback.
  • On cash and leverage, Sanderson said, "we had $36 million in cash and a total debt of $182 million at the end of the quarter, resulting in a net leverage ratio of 1.5x," and added, "Cash used in operating activities was $12 million" and "Total CapEx was $19 million, including $8.4 million for our Arkansas bromine project."
  • Sanderson attributed Q1 cash use to working capital and compensation items: "The use of cash was driven by higher incentive compensation tied to our strong 2025 financial results" and also reflected "a build in our -- AR balance" and "the seasonal inventory build in Europe, which will be monetized in Q2."

Q&A

  • Bobby Brooks, Northland Capital Markets: Asked what "multiple engineering efforts and customer commercial engagements" means for OASIS and how it compares to six months ago; (President Murphy) said, "we actually now have several parallel engineering studies going on for a smaller-sized plant as well as a 100,000-barrel-per-day plant," and expects to get "into more commercial discussions with our customers before the end of the second quarter," while cautioning, "we're in the middle of engineering studies... before we can really get into any long-term contracts."
  • Bobby Brooks, Northland Capital Markets: Asked whether inbound interest has increased since Investor Day; (Executive VP Sanderson) said, "those engagements, those dialogues, those engineering studies... that's increased" and called OASIS "very cost competitive with alternatives" as disposal costs rise.
  • Martin Malloy, Johnson Rice: Asked about Neptune timing and deepwater demand; (President Murphy) said, "the pipeline continues to grow" and "the probabilities for next year are continuing to increase pretty significantly," while noting some projects may be pulled forward.
  • Martin Malloy, Johnson Rice: Asked whether lithium/magnesium acceleration depends on bromine; (President Murphy) said, "That project is our priority," and added that infrastructure for bromine will support future plants, but "We're not ready to publish any financial information on those projects yet."
  • Timothy Michael Moore, Clear Street: Asked about electrolyte supply/logistics related to EOS; (President Murphy) said, "we're very plugged into their forecast" but "we really can't talk about any specifics," adding, "we have secured additional third-party bromine supply that is in place to meet the forecast."
  • Timothy Michael Moore, Clear Street: Asked about Arkansas bromine milestones and CapEx; (President Murphy) said Phase 1 is complete and emphasized, "standing the bromine tower up... was a logistics challenge," while noting additional construction remains and, "we are looking to finance as much of that as we can from our free cash flow."
  • Patrick Ouellette, Stifel: Asked about magnesium JV scale and demand; (President Murphy) said the JV is finalized and "a little premature" to size a commercial plant, citing the need to evaluate "offtake agreements" and potential "government funding."
  • Patrick Ouellette, Stifel: Asked about translating deepwater rigs to 2027 fluids demand; (Executive VP Sanderson) referenced prior commentary that 2026 shifts into more drilling and 2027 back toward completions, adding that deeper, hotter projects require "higher density brines and more exotic chemistries."
  • Jonathan Tanwanteng, CJS Securities: Asked about Standard Lithium/Equinor drilling and TETRA well needs; (President Murphy) clarified, "The wells that we drill... contain both lithium bromine and magnesium," and added, "We're not at a point where we're ready to FID a lithium plant."
  • Jonathan Tanwanteng, CJS Securities: Asked about calcium chloride market impacts from conflict; (President Murphy) said, "We're not -- I wouldn't say we're seeing any material change due to the current conflict," adding, "our European business is very strong" and "our U.S. business is very strong."
  • Josh Jayne, Daniel Energy Partners: Asked about international production testing mix and geopolitical changes; (Executive VP Sanderson) reiterated expectations to "double revenue in Argentina in 2026" and emphasized exporting automation such as SandStorm into international markets.
  • Josh Jayne, Daniel Energy Partners: Asked about incremental conversations tied to energy security; (President Murphy) pointed to offshore deepwater and unconventional U.S./Argentina as priority markets, while (Executive VP Sanderson) added there are "increased inquiry of spot sale" requests tied to bromine sourcing outside the Middle East.
  • Bobby Brooks, Northland Capital Markets: Asked about U.S. onshore completion fluids outlook; (President Murphy) said land is smaller for TETRA but cited growth in "South Texas" and "the Western Haynesville" and workover needs in West Texas due to rising "poor pressures."
  • Martin Malloy, Johnson Rice: Asked about Argentina services mix; (Executive VP Sanderson) said it is "a little bit of a combination" of early production facilities and SandStorm deployment in Vaca Muerta.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive, with multiple questions framed around scaling opportunities and timelines, including "Congratulations on a solid quarter" (Martin Malloy, Johnson Rice) and repeated probing on OASIS, Neptune probability, and critical minerals execution details.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in Q&A on customer specifics, with caveats such as "we cannot disclose the customers" (Executive VP Sanderson) and "we really can't talk about any specifics" (President Murphy) when discussing EOS forecasting and commercial counterparties.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management commentary carried more emphasis on geopolitics and logistics timing, while maintaining prior guidance language; analysts continued focusing on OASIS scale-up and bromine supply dynamics, consistent with the previous call’s core themes.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Leadership and structure shifted, with Sanderson now serving as CFO in Q1 2026 after the prior quarter’s planned transition from retiring CFO Elijio Serrano.
  • The strategic narrative remained consistent around One TETRA 2030, but Q1 2026 added sharper near-term framing around conflict-driven shifts, including potential Middle East sales delays and an uptick in spot-sale inquiries outside historical regions.
  • OASIS discussion evolved from prior-quarter timing targets toward parallel engineering tracks, as Murphy said Q1 includes "several parallel engineering studies" spanning smaller units and a "100,000-barrel-per-day plant," versus the prior call’s emphasis on scaling from 25,000 to 100,000-plus and earlier commercialization timing discussions.

Risks and concerns

  • Sanderson cited operational risk from geopolitics and logistics: "offshore activity in the Middle East has slowed, and logistics into the region continue to face higher costs and shipping delays," with potential for Q2 sales delays.
  • Murphy emphasized commodity-price uncertainty: "significant uncertainty remains for oil and gas prices," while describing potential activity responses at higher prices.
  • Execution and timing risk remains on large growth projects, with Murphy stating OASIS requires completion of engineering work "before we can really get into any long-term contracts," and lithium development remains pre-FID with "more technology evaluation and engineering studies" needed.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a decade-high start (excluding prior-year Neptune timing), kept full-year 2026 guidance unchanged, and framed geopolitics as a manageable exposure with possible Middle East timing delays offset by activity and spot inquiries elsewhere. The call emphasized that Arkansas bromine capacity and timing remain central to supplying deepwater fluids and battery electrolyte growth, while OASIS desalination moved into multiple parallel engineering studies aimed at enabling broader commercial discussions by the end of Q2.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nelnet acquires Australia-based Invision Digital
data.description:
• Nelnet Business Services, a division of Nelnet (NNI) on Thursday said it has acquired Australia-based Invision Digital Pty Ltd, the owner of the Passtab, Resitab, and Entrytab brands.
  • The acquired products — Passtab, a school visitor, contractor and compliance management platform, plus Resitab and Entrytab — will operate within the Nelnet International business line.
  • Press release
